The strongest teacher solution keeps the full workflow staged from arrays to table
summaries to figures so students can see why each library is doing a different part
of the job.

## Suggested approach

1. Load and sort the CSV first so the table and the line plot share a clear order.
2. Pull the numeric columns into NumPy arrays before adding new table columns so the
   vectorized calculations stay visible.
3. Build the grouped Pandas summary explicitly with named aggregate columns.
4. Use separate Matplotlib figures for the line, scatter, and bar plots so the
   comparison between plot types stays easy to read.
5. Save the final report figure from the same explicit plotting code used on screen.

## Minimal reference implementation

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.read_csv(
    "assets/campus_store_performance.csv",
    parse_dates=["date"],
).sort_values("date")
print(sales.head())
print(sales.dtypes)

orders = sales["orders"].to_numpy()
revenue = sales["revenue"].to_numpy()
marketing = sales["marketing_spend"].to_numpy()
print(orders.shape)
print(revenue.shape)
print(marketing.shape)

average_order_value = revenue / orders
print(np.round(average_order_value[:5], 2))
print(np.mean(average_order_value))
print(np.std(average_order_value))

revenue_matrix = revenue[:6].reshape((2, 3))
print(revenue_matrix)
print(revenue_matrix.sum(axis=1))

sales["average_order_value"] = average_order_value
print(sales.loc[:, ["date", "region", "channel", "average_order_value"]])

online_high_value = sales[
    (sales["channel"] == "online") & (sales["average_order_value"] >= 41)
]
print(online_high_value)

region_summary = (
    sales.groupby("region")
    .agg(
        total_revenue=("revenue", "sum"),
        total_orders=("orders", "sum"),
        mean_average_order_value=("average_order_value", "mean"),
    )
    .sort_values(by="total_revenue", ascending=False)
)
print(region_summary)

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(sales["date"], sales["revenue"], marker="o")
plt.title("Revenue over time")
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.scatter(sales["marketing_spend"], sales["revenue"])
plt.title("Marketing spend versus revenue")
plt.xlabel("Marketing spend")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.bar(region_summary.index, region_summary["total_revenue"])
plt.title("Total revenue by region")
plt.xlabel("Region")
plt.ylabel("Total revenue")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.plot(sales["date"], sales["revenue"], marker="o")
plt.title("Revenue over time")
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Revenue")
plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("campus_store_revenue_report.png")
plt.show()
```

## What students should notice

- NumPy makes the column-level arithmetic compact and explicit.
- Pandas makes it easy to move from row-level data to grouped regional summaries.
- The same underlying table can answer different questions once the plot type changes.
- Saving the final figure is just one more reproducible step in the workflow.

## Teaching emphasis

Keep the emphasis on sequence: inspect the table, extract arrays, create the derived
metric, summarize by region, then plot. The goal is to show how the lecture 6-8
stack works as one small reporting pipeline.
